name: Copy Into a New Matrix
summary: Place each element into its rotated position in a second matrix, then copy that back.
visualizationIntent: init: the source matrix is shown and an empty rotated matrix pops in beside it; loop(fill): the read cell matrix[row][col] is current and its rotated destination rotated[col][n-1-row] flashes as it receives the value; loop(copy): the second pass copies rotated back into matrix cell by cell; return: matrix holds the rotated image.

lineExplanations.3: Measure n, the number of rows and columns.
lineExplanations.4: Start an empty list that will become the rotated matrix.
lineExplanations.5: Repeat n times to build n rows.
lineExplanations.6: Append a fresh row of n zeros.
lineExplanations.9: Place matrix[row][col] into its rotated spot rotated[col][n-1-row].
lineExplanations.10: Walk the rows again for the copy-back pass.
lineExplanations.12: Copy each rotated value back into matrix, in place.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: the wrapper class the platform runs.
syntaxExplanations.2: def rotate(self, matrix): the method receiving the n x n matrix.
syntaxExplanations.3: len(matrix) is the number of rows, n.
syntaxExplanations.5: for _ in range(n) repeats n times; _ marks an unused index.
syntaxExplanations.6: [0] * n makes a row of n zeros; .append adds it.
syntaxExplanations.7: for row in range(n) iterates the row indices.
syntaxExplanations.8: for col in range(n) iterates the column indices.
syntaxExplanations.9: n - 1 - row is the destination column for a clockwise rotation.
syntaxExplanations.10: for row in range(n) begins the second pass.
syntaxExplanations.11: for col in range(n) iterates columns again.
syntaxExplanations.12: matrix[row][col] = rotated[row][col] writes back in place.
syntaxExplanations.13: return matrix hands back the rotated grid.
